title: Hurricanes and tropical storms
summary: What to do before, during, and after a hurricane. Adapted from official Ready.gov, National Hurricane Center (NHC), and Red Cross guidance. If authorities order an evacuation, evacuate.

antes.heading: Know your zone and your evacuation route
antes.body: Check with civil protection whether your area is an evacuation zone and which way out to take. Decide now where you would go: a relative's home inland, or the nearest official shelter. Evacuating late, on packed roads, is the most dangerous option.

antes.heading: Stock supplies for several days
antes.body: Water (1 gallon per person per day), non-perishable food, medications, flashlight, radio, and cash for at least 3 days — 7 if you can. After a major hurricane, services can take weeks to return.

antes.heading: Protect your home
antes.body: Board up windows, secure or store anything the wind can turn into a projectile (planters, loose metal roofing, patio furniture), and clear gutters and drains.

antes.heading: Full tank and documents ready
antes.body: Fill the vehicle's tank early and keep important documents in a waterproof bag. Charge phones and power banks before the wind arrives.

antes.heading: If ordered to evacuate: evacuate
antes.body: Evacuation orders are issued because the area is not survivable in storm surge or flooding. Don't wait to 'see how it goes'. Leave early, tell your out-of-area contact, and follow official routes.

durante.heading: Stay inside, away from windows
durante.body: Shelter in an interior room without windows, on the lowest level that won't flood. Hurricane winds throw debris at lethal speed — most injuries during the storm are from glass and flying objects.

durante.heading: The eye of the hurricane deceives
durante.body: If the wind suddenly calms, it may be the eye: the second half of the storm arrives within minutes, with wind from the opposite direction. Do not go out until officials announce the storm has fully passed.

durante.heading: If water starts coming in
durante.body: Shut off electricity at the main breaker if it's safe to reach, and move to the highest level where you won't be trapped (not a closed attic with no exit). Take your phone, radio, and whistle.

durante.heading: Never drive or walk through moving water
durante.body: Twelve inches of water can move a car; six inches can knock a person down. Most hurricane deaths are from drowning in water, not wind. Turn around.

durante.heading: If you're caught outside
durante.body: Get to the nearest solid structure immediately. Stay away from trees, poles, and power lines. A ditch or low ground is the last resort against extreme wind.

despues.heading: Wait for the official all-clear before returning
despues.body: Do not return to an evacuated area until authorities announce it. Roads may be cut, energized by downed lines, or undermined by water.

despues.heading: Generators OUTDOORS only
despues.body: Never run a generator, grill, or gas stove inside the house or garage, or near windows. Carbon monoxide is invisible and odorless and kills entire families after storms. Outdoors only, away from openings.

despues.heading: Beware of water and power lines
despues.body: Treat every downed line as energized. Floodwater may be contaminated with sewage or fuel — don't use it for anything, and discard any food it touched.

despues.heading: Document damage and report it
despues.body: Photograph damage before cleaning up, for insurance and aid. If your structure is compromised, request an assessment through Vigil and don't occupy it until it's checked.

supplyChecklist.qty: 1 gal per person per day × 7 days
supplyChecklist.note: Also fill bathtubs and containers before the storm
For NHC and civil-protection notices without power
